question: A patient undergoes contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen in the portal venous phase. Based on the imaging, which of the following pharmacologic therapies is most appropriate?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the provided axial contrast-enhanced CT image in the portal venous phase. The white arrow points to a central low-attenuation filling defect within the superior mesenteric vein at the level of the pancreatic head, with contrast visible around the defect, indicating a partial (non-occlusive) venous thrombus on this slice. I do not see clear radiographic signs of bowel infarction on this image (no obvious pneumatosis or gross bowel wall discontinuity visible here).

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the question, which asks which pharmacologic therapy is most appropriate for the pictured lesion. The image documents an established venous thrombosis of the superior mesenteric vein that requires therapeutic anticoagulation rather than only prophylaxis or antiplatelet therapy.

Then I consider the options in light of the image: systemic thrombolysis (B) is typically reserved for extensive occlusion with imaging or clinical evidence of acute catastrophic ischemia, which is not supported by this image showing a non-occlusive thrombus without clear ischemic change. Antiplatelet therapy (C) and prophylactic-dose heparin (D) are aimed at arterial disease or prevention and are not adequate for treating an established venous thrombus seen here. Immediate direct oral anticoagulant monotherapy (E) is an anticoagulant option, but among the choices the one explicitly providing immediate therapeutic parenteral anticoagulation is therapeutic low-molecular-weight heparin (A), which matches the need to treat an acute mesenteric venous thrombosis.

Therefore, based on the CT appearance of a confirmed superior mesenteric vein thrombus requiring therapeutic anticoagulation, therapeutic low-molecular-weight heparin is the most appropriate choice.
caption: Axial image of a computed tomography (CT) of the abdomen with intravenous and oral contrast, in the portal venous phase. A non-occlusive thrombus can be seen within the superior mesenteric vein at the level of the pancreatic head (shown by the arrow).
sub_caption: Axial image of a computed tomography (CT) of the abdomen with intravenous and oral contrast, in the portal venous phase. A non-occlusive thrombus can be seen within the superior mesenteric vein at the level of the pancreatic head (shown by the arrow).
image_context: The patient was treated with antibiotics for presumed infection, pending a follow-up appointment with gastroenterology. However, over the following 5 weeks, the patient attended the emergency department on 2 more occasions with similar symptoms. A second intravenous and oral contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed (<xref rid="radcr283-fig-0003" ref-type="fig">Fig. 3</xref>, , <xref rid="radcr283-fig-0004" ref-type="fig">Fig. 4</xref>), 40 days after the initial CT examination. This revealed resolution of the previous jejunal wall thickening and a reduction in the inflammatory changes in the adjacent mesentery, although minimal stranding and smaller mesenteric lymph nodes persisted. Of note, there were new filling defects consistent with thrombosis in the splenic vein adjacent to the splenic hilum, in the superior mesenteric vein at the junction of the superior mesenteric and portal veins, in the main portal vein, and in the left portal vein. The liver demonstrated a heterogenous attenuation with poorly defined hypodensities in the right and left lobes. No other abnormalities were evident.), 40 days after the initial CT examination. The liver demonstrated a heterogenous attenuation with poorly defined hypodensities in the right and left lobes.
